references.notes: The phrase 'ilāf Allāh' refers to a divinely sanctioned covenant or protection, explicitly naming 'Allāh' as a pre-Islamic high god.
The verse contains an invocation to 'Rabbat al-ʿAyr,' a female entity, possibly a local deity or spirit, for assistance, which is characteristic of pre-Islamic appeals to divine powers.

This verse personifies death (al-radā) as an agent that uses the vicissitudes of fate (ṣurūf) to bring about appointed death (ḥimām), reflecting pre-Islamic cosmological concepts of fate and time as active forces.
The poet expresses the inevitability of death by stating he will join ancient peoples like ʿĀd, Nūḥ, and Sām, reflecting the pre-Islamic concept of death as joining the deceased ancestors.
This verse contains an explicit oath sworn by Allah, which was a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia, anchoring the oath to a sacred entity.
The term 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) is invoked as a force that can 'darken its face,' reflecting the pre-Islamic Arabian concept of Dahr as a cosmological, often malevolent, force of fate.
The verse personifies 'dahr' (time/fate) as an active, destructive force, which aligns with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about dahr as an agent of destiny.
The verse refers to 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) as actively preparing arrows, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of fate as an irresistible, personified force.
